issueModules.title: No valid certificate of occupancy bars rent (MDL 301/302)
issueModules.when: Building/unit occupied contrary to its certificate of occupancy (e.g., illegal combination or subdivision of units, commercial space converted to apartments). Open DOB/ECB/OATH violations stating 'occupancy contrary to c/o' are prima facie evidence the c/o violation exists. Bar on rent applies building-wide, not just to the altered unit, and the Chazon rule is strict — no equitable/practical exception, no requirement the violation affect the specific unit or structural integrity
issueModules.strength: STRONG — Court of Appeals (Chazon) + multiple App Term & Civ Ct decisions; appellate support (GVS Vargas, 49 Bleecker, Pei-De Tsai)

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (landlord-favorable): when an MDL 301/302 c/o defense FAILS
issueModules.when: The c/o defense can fail where (a) the building has a valid I-card that serves the function of a c/o and the change (e.g., number of bedrooms) does not require a new c/o, and there is no actual outstanding c/o violation — only the tenant's supposition (Agresti); (b) the c/o issue was already litigated and decided, so law of the case / collateral estoppel bars relitigation (Agresti); or (c) the tenant simply stopped paying all rent and a DHCR order already fixed the legal regulated rent with arrears exceeding any overcharge (Cornine — ejectment in Supreme Court). Note Agresti and Cornine were not summary nonpayment proceedings
issueModules.strength: trial-level / Sup Ct counterweights to the c/o defense
issueModules.posture: UNFAVORABLE — anticipate and rebut: confirm an actual open c/o violation exists (not just an I-card or supposition), avoid having the issue decided adversely earlier, and don't let the client cease all rent payments without a deposit strategy

issueModules.title: Notice of Petition fails to use mandatory court form
issueModules.when: NoP does not conform to 22 NYCRR 208.42(b) / AO 163/19
issueModules.strength: moderate (trial-level, growing; verify)

issueModules.title: Overcharge discovery for post-HSTPA tenancies
issueModules.when: Tenancy began after 6/14/2019 and rent history looks unreliable
issueModules.strength: first dept appellate (Harvey)

issueModules.title: Rent-impairing violations -> up to 100% abatement / no rent recoverable (MDL 302-a)
issueModules.when: Open HPD rent-impairing violation (order number marked with an asterisk; e.g., order 507/567 roof leak, rodent infestation) uncorrected more than six months after DHPD mailed notice to the owner. A common-area or owner-controlled-area violation (roof, courtyard) is deemed to exist in each resident's unit, and the landlord's claim that the tenant denied access to the apartment will NOT rebut the defense where the condition is in a common area
issueModules.strength: trial-level, multiple aligned decisions; pairs with habitability

issueModules.title: MDL 302-a three-element test + the proof-of-mailing trap
issueModules.when: Building any 302-a rent-impairing defense. Tenant must affirmatively plead AND prove: (1) a deposit of the amount due under the petition (including amendments) with the court/escrow; (2) a rent-impairing violation not certified as corrected within six months of notice; and (3) proof that DHPD mailed the notice of violation to the owner. Edwards is the cautionary case — the tenant LOST despite open violations because the subpoenaed DHPD bulk-mailing records were a disorganized pile that failed to prove mailing of the specific violations. Subpoena DHPD directly for proof of service of the specific NOVs; don't rely on records from another case
issueModules.strength: trial-level framework; Edwards is a published cautionary opinion
issueModules.posture: NEUTRAL — framework/checklist; the proof-of-mailing element is where 302-a defenses fail

issueModules.title: Farkas six-factor ample-need discovery test
issueModules.when: Building a discovery motion in a summary proceeding
issueModules.strength: framework reference

issueModules.title: GCEL non-renewal holdover grounded on nonpayment requires a notice stating a good-faith amount owed, the months owed, and the increase percentage
issueModules.when: Good Cause non-renewal holdover where the 'good cause' is nonpayment, and the 90-day termination notice omits the rent amount, the periods owed, or the percentage of any increase — defect precludes the tenant's pre-warrant cure right and requires dismissal
issueModules.strength: trial-level; multiple Civ. Ct. decisions aligning (verify currency)

issueModules.title: Domestic-violence / mental-health incapacity can supply a reasonable excuse to vacate a default under CPLR 5015(a)(1)
issueModules.when: Respondent defaulted while incapacitated by trauma/DV (e.g., hospitalization, PTSD, depression with medical documentation), now has counsel and a potentially meritorious defense
issueModules.strength: trial-level; applies the standard 5015 two-prong test

issueModules.title: GCEL (RPL 216) creates no independent cause of action; early-termination holdovers still require a conditional limitation, not a mere condition subsequent
issueModules.when: Holdover premised on a lease option-to-terminate clause (condition subsequent) rather than an automatic conditional limitation; or landlord argues the Good Cause statute itself authorizes the holdover. A bare option-to-terminate gives only ejectment under RPAPL art. 6, not a summary proceeding
issueModules.strength: trial-level (applies 3d Dept law); persuasive in NYC — verify against 1st/2d Dept

issueModules.title: Fraud exception to the rent-overcharge lookback needs only 'sufficient indicia of fraud' or a 'colorable claim' — not every element of common-law fraud; post-2023 amendments restore the Thornton/Grimm 'totality of the circumstances' analysis
issueModules.when: Rent overcharge claim where landlord invokes the four-year (or RSL 26-516(a)) lookback to bar review. The 2023/2024 RSL/RSC amendments (L 2023 ch 760; L 2024 ch 95) apply to all claims pending as of 12/22/2023, direct courts back to Thornton v Baron and Grimm, permit consideration of records of any age, and use a 'totality of the circumstances' test for whether the owner 'knowingly engaged' in a fraudulent deregulation scheme. Tenant need only show indicia of fraud / a colorable claim to unlock review beyond the base date
issueModules.strength: STRONG — Court of Appeals (Aras applying Burrows; Thornton; Grimm; Liggett) + App Term (Goldstein)

issueModules.title: Rent-control status persists through temporary exemption; regulatory status cannot be conferred by waiver/estoppel; FMRA four-year bar does not apply when regulatory status itself is at issue
issueModules.when: Overcharge/regulatory-status dispute where the apartment was historically rent-controlled. A rent-controlled apartment retains that status even if it becomes temporarily exempt or subject to a program that would otherwise confer stabilization; coverage cannot be created by waiver or estoppel; and the four-year limit on a fair market rent appeal does not apply where the dispute is about regulatory status. Initial stabilized rent after rent control is set under RSC 2521.1(a)(1) (Liggett). Note: in Nebbou both sides' summary judgment motions were DENIED — fact issues on prior status
issueModules.strength: trial-level framework citing 1st Dept and Court of Appeals authority

issueModules.title: Succession to a regulated tenancy: co-residency + family-member status (traditional or nontraditional)
issueModules.when: Holdover/licensee proceeding against an occupant claiming succession. Successor must show (a) a qualifying family relationship — traditional (e.g., son under 9 NYCRR 2204.6(d)(3)(i)) or nontraditional via the Braschi emotional-and-financial-commitment/interdependence factors — and (b) primary co-residency with the tenant of record for 2 years (1 year if the successor is elderly or disabled) before the tenant's permanent vacatur. WINS: Sequin (disabled son, 1-year co-residency, BOE/SSA/utility records; nursing-home vacatur did not defeat it; landlord offered no documentary rebuttal). LOSSES to distinguish: Ordonez (nontraditional claim failed — 'one-sided,' asymmetric relationship, large age gap, tenant named family not claimant in his documents) and Marzullo (failed to prove the requisite co-residency period before the parent's death; also barred by successive-SJ rule)
issueModules.strength: Court of Appeals standard (Braschi) applied at trial/App Term level
issueModules.posture: MIXED — favorable framework (Sequin) with two landlord-favorable cautionary examples (Ordonez, Marzullo)

issueModules.title: Sublet/short-term-hosting profiteering is a CURABLE violation unless the tenant charged ABOVE a proportionate share
issueModules.when: Holdover alleging illegal profiteering from subtenants/short-term guests. Charging a subtenant/guest more than the tenant's own proportionate share of the rent for the days hosted is incurable profiteering (Goldstein v Lipetz line). But where the per-diem charged did NOT exceed the tenant's share of rent for the days actually hosting, it is not incurable and does not warrant forfeiture without an opportunity to cure — especially for a long-term, elderly, regulated tenant (Reynolds: 85-year-old, 40-year tenancy, charged at or below proportionate share)
issueModules.strength: App Term, 1st Dept

issueModules.title: Tenant harassment counterclaim (HMC 27-2004/27-2005(d)): buyout pressure + repeated failure to correct hazardous violations
issueModules.when: Landlord's agents repeatedly pressured the tenant to vacate / made buyout offers (Guzman: repeated demands and buyout offers = harassment under 27-2004(a)(48)(f-2)), or the landlord repeatedly failed to correct hazardous/immediately-hazardous violations within the required time (Phillip). Remedies: HPD Class 'C' violation, civil penalties (27-2115(m)(2): not less than $2,000), injunction, compensatory damages, possible punitive damages and attorneys' fees (27-2115(o)). CAUTION (Guzman): where the lawful rent is zero because of a c/o bar, there is no baseline to measure a rent-abatement-style compensatory/habitability award, so the court may award only nominal/statutory compensatory damages
issueModules.strength: trial-level; consistent Civ Ct application

issueModules.title: PRACTICE TRAP: an amended answer supersedes the original — defenses/counterclaims not carried forward are WAIVED
issueModules.when: Any time you amend an answer. The amended answer supersedes the prior pleading in all respects (Keary). In Edwards, the amended answer dropped the habitability defense/counterclaim and kept only the 302-a defense, so the tenant could not get a habitability abatement. Always re-plead every defense and counterclaim you intend to keep in the amended answer
issueModules.strength: settled appellate rule (Keary), illustrated in Edwards

issueModules.title: An amended answer in a summary proceeding may be verified by counsel (or unverified)
issueModules.when: Landlord objects that the amended answer is verified by the attorney rather than the respondent. RPAPL 743 allows a respondent to answer orally or in an unverified written answer, and no authority requires amended answers to be treated differently
issueModules.strength: trial-level

issueModules.title: Specific factual denial of service rebuts the affidavit and warrants a traverse hearing
issueModules.when: Challenging service. A bare, conclusory denial of receipt is insufficient to rebut a process server's affidavit (and an unsworn denial in a memo of law, or new facts raised first in a reply affirmation, won't do it). But a specific factual denial — e.g., that the papers were left near the building intercom rather than affixed to the door — raises an issue of fact requiring a traverse hearing (Davidson). Affix-and-mail service under CPLR 308(4) requires genuine due diligence first (Davidson found five attempts over two months sufficient)
issueModules.strength: App Term, 1st Dept
issueModules.posture: FAVORABLE — but plead service defenses with specific sworn facts

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (landlord-favorable): retaliatory eviction must be pleaded; good-faith nonpayment basis defeats it
issueModules.when: Retaliatory-eviction defense. It is unpreserved if raised for the first time on appeal and not in the (amended) answer (Bei Yang). It also does not apply where the landlord acted in good faith on a legitimate ground such as nonpayment of rent/maintenance. Constructive eviction and excessive-noise habitability claims turn on credibility and require the condition to deprive the tenant of essential residential functions
issueModules.strength: App Term, 1st Dept
issueModules.posture: UNFAVORABLE — plead retaliation in the answer with specific facts; anticipate a good-faith rebuttal

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (landlord-favorable): a single sufficiently serious incident can constitute nuisance
issueModules.when: Nuisance holdover. There is no strict quantitative test; a court weighs both quantitative and qualitative aspects, and one incident serious enough (in Gray, a shooting in the apartment) may survive a motion to dismiss as a cognizable nuisance claim. On a 3211 motion the court takes the notice of termination's allegations as true
issueModules.strength: trial-level (2004); illustrative
issueModules.posture: UNFAVORABLE — distinguish by isolating the incident, showing it is non-recurring and not part of an ongoing pattern

issueModules.title: Where a deceased tenant of record's unexpired lease was in effect at commencement, the estate is a necessary party and the failure to name it warrants dismissal
issueModules.when: Holdover (including licensee) against an occupant after the tenant of record died, where an unexpired lease was still in effect when the case was commenced but the estate / a Surrogate's-appointed representative was not named. Note: court may order joinder (CPLR 1003) rather than dismiss — fact-dependent
issueModules.strength: trial-level; outcome turns on whether the lease was genuinely unexpired

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (landlord-favorable): a missing estate may NOT be a necessary party where the CPLR 1001(b) five discretionary factors favor proceeding without joinder
issueModules.when: You move to dismiss for non-joinder of an estate, but the absent party's interests are not immediately/inequitably affected (e.g., no immediate sale of the home, and the movant could just as easily seek appointment of a representative)
issueModules.strength: trial-level counterweight to the necessary-party defense
issueModules.posture: UNFAVORABLE — distinguish by showing the absent party IS immediately/inequitably affected and an unexpired lease exists (cf. Calvary/Hicks)

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (landlord-favorable): a belief that the papers were 'fake,' without diligent inquiry, is not a reasonable excuse to vacate a default
issueModules.when: Respondent seeks to vacate a default claiming they thought the summons was illegitimate, but had received prior demand letters / court correspondence and made no real inquiry. (Frederick was a civil rent-debt action, but the 5015 reasoning applies to summary proceedings too)
issueModules.strength: trial-level (Bronx); landlord-favorable on excusable default
issueModules.posture: UNFAVORABLE — distinguish by showing diligent inquiry, no prior notice, or by pairing with a strong meritorious defense

issueModules.title: HOME Program affordability period is a 30-year minimum, not a maximum (HDFC co-op resale restrictions)
issueModules.when: Niche / contextual: an HDFC cooperative seeks to escape resale/affordability restrictions by arguing the federal HOME Program affordability period was only 15 years. Court held the regulation sets a MINIMUM, the recorded Regulatory Agreement / offering plan / proprietary lease controlled a 30-year period, and DHCR/HTFC retain approval authority until expiry. Low direct relevance to tenant defense in summary proceedings
issueModules.strength: trial-level (Sup Ct); niche
